content.text: Representational State Transfer (REST) is an architectural style for designing networked applications, formalized by Roy Fielding in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It defines a set of constraints that, when applied to a web service, produce properties like scalability, simplicity, and interoperability — the same properties that made the World Wide Web itself so successful at scale.

REST is not a protocol, a standard, or a library. It is a set of six architectural constraints that describe how a well-designed distributed hypermedia system should behave. When a web service conforms to all six constraints, it is described as RESTful. In practice, most production APIs adopt five of the six constraints (skipping "Code on Demand"), and the term REST is used loosely to mean any HTTP-based API that follows the resource-oriented conventions.

The term REST was coined by Roy Fielding, one of the primary authors of the HTTP specification, to describe the principles he observed in the design of the web. His insight was that the web's remarkable scalability and decentralization were not accidental — they emerged directly from a small set of deliberate constraints applied to how clients and servers communicate.

A REST API exposes application state and functionality as a set of resources, each addressable by a stable URI. Clients manipulate resources through a small, fixed set of operations — the standard HTTP methods — and the server responds with a representation of the resource in a negotiated format (typically JSON or XML).

Before REST-based web APIs became mainstream, the dominant approach for remote procedure calls was SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol). SOAP requires detailed XML envelopes, service-specific operation names, and WSDL definition files. Integration between systems required extensive code generation and negotiation, and every service invented its own vocabulary for operations.

REST's uniform interface constraint solves this by insisting that all resources be manipulated through a shared protocol vocabulary — HTTP verbs plus standard status codes. A developer who understands HTTP already understands how to interact with any RESTful API. The cognitive overhead of integrating a new service drops from reading a WSDL to reading a short resource reference.

Scalability concerns are addressed by the stateless constraint. When the server holds no session state between requests, any server instance can handle any request from any client. Horizontal scaling — adding more server nodes — becomes trivial because there is no session affinity problem to solve. Load balancers can route requests freely across a fleet of identical stateless servers.

Cacheability improves network efficiency: because REST responses are explicitly labeled as cacheable or non-cacheable, CDNs, proxies, and browsers can safely cache responses. A large portion of read traffic never reaches origin servers at all, reducing latency and compute cost.
Clients and servers agree only on the protocol (HTTP) and the resource representation (JSON). Neither needs to know the other's internal implementation.
With no per-client session state on the server, scaling from one node to a hundred is purely an infrastructure task, not an application redesign.
) allow clients, proxies, and CDNs to serve repeated reads from local copies.
API gateways, load balancers, security proxies, and CDNs can be inserted between client and server without either side needing to change.
REST uses standard HTTP, so requests and responses are inspectable with any HTTP tool — curl, browser dev tools, Postman, or a simple network proxy.
Any programming language with an HTTP library can consume or serve a REST API, making it the universal integration interface.
HTTP status codes, methods, and headers carry shared semantics, reducing the documentation burden. A 404 means "not found" in every API.

REST resources return fixed payloads. A client that only needs a user's name receives the entire user object. A client that needs a user plus their posts must make two separate requests. GraphQL was designed specifically to address this limitation.
Statelessness shifts complexity to the client.

Every request must carry authentication tokens, session context, and any other state the server needs to fulfill it. This makes requests larger and requires careful token management on the client side.
Multiple round trips for related resources.

Without request batching, rendering a page that needs five related resources requires five network round trips. HTTP/2 multiplexing reduces the latency cost, but does not eliminate the server-processing overhead.

REST's constraints are informal — nothing prevents a developer from making a DELETE endpoint return 200 with an error body, or from tunneling all operations through POST. The REST label does not guarantee a well-designed API.
Not ideal for streaming or real-time data.
REST's request/response model does not naturally support server-push. Long-polling, Server-Sent Events, or WebSockets are needed for real-time use cases, and they sit outside the core REST model.

As an API evolves, maintaining backward compatibility while introducing breaking changes requires careful versioning strategies (URI versioning, header versioning, content negotiation), each with different trade-offs.

REST operates on top of HTTP, using the protocol's existing mechanisms for all communication. The client constructs an HTTP request identifying a resource by URI, specifying an operation via the HTTP method, and optionally including a representation in the body. The server processes the request and returns an HTTP response with a status code and optionally a representation.

The HTTP methods map directly to resource operations. GET retrieves a representation without modifying server state. POST creates a new resource subordinate to the target URI. PUT replaces the target resource with a new complete representation. PATCH applies a partial update to the resource. DELETE removes the target resource.

Status codes communicate the outcome unambiguously. The 2xx class signals success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content). The 3xx class indicates redirection. The 4xx class means the client sent a request with an error it is responsible for (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 422 Unprocessable Entity). The 5xx class indicates a server-side failure.
Content negotiation allows the same resource to serve multiple representations. The client sends an
header specifying the formats it prefers (
), and the server selects the best match, indicating its choice in the

Idempotency is a critical property that REST inherits from HTTP semantics. An operation is idempotent if making it N times has the same effect as making it once. GET, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent; POST is not. Clients use idempotency to safely retry failed requests without the risk of duplicate side effects.

The six REST constraints define the architecture at a high level. The diagram below shows how they relate to the request flow from client to resource store, with the Uniform Interface governing every interaction across the wire.

attrs.alt: REST Principles architecture diagram — six constraints and request flow

content.text: The user interface (client) is separated from data storage (server). The client is not concerned with data storage; the server is not concerned with the user interface. This separation allows each to evolve independently — a mobile app can change its UI without touching the server, and the server can change its storage layer without touching any client.

Each request from client to server must contain all the information necessary to understand and process the request. The server must not store any client session state between requests. This means authentication credentials (tokens), pagination cursors, and any other session context must travel with every request, not be stored in server memory.

Responses must label themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable. If a response is cacheable, the client or an intermediate cache is entitled to reuse the response data for later equivalent requests. The HTTP

This is the central distinguishing constraint of REST. It has four sub-constraints: resource identification through URIs; manipulation of resources through representations; self-descriptive messages (enough metadata to describe how to process the message); and hypermedia as the engine of application state (HATEOAS — responses include links to related actions).

Intermediary components (load balancers, caches, security proxies, API gateways) can be inserted between client and server. Neither client nor server needs to know how many intermediary layers exist. Each layer can only see the layer immediately adjacent to it.

Servers can extend client functionality by sending executable code — JavaScript, for instance — that the client can run. This is the only optional constraint and is the standard mechanism by which web browsers work. Most pure REST API designs do not use this constraint.

REST is a strong choice when building public or partner-facing APIs. Its use of standard HTTP makes it accessible to any developer with basic HTTP knowledge. Documentation tooling (Swagger/OpenAPI) is mature, and client libraries can be auto-generated from specifications.

REST fits CRUD-heavy workloads well. When the primary operations on the API map to creating, reading, updating, and deleting persistent entities (users, orders, products, documents), REST's resource-oriented model is a natural fit.

REST is appropriate when caching is a key performance requirement. A news feed, product catalogue, or reference data API can push a large fraction of read traffic into CDN or browser caches using standard HTTP cache headers.

when clients have highly variable data requirements and over-fetching is a performance concern — a mobile client that needs a strict subset of a large resource graph benefits significantly from GraphQL's field selection.

for internal microservice communication where you need strong typing, bi-directional streaming, and lower serialization overhead. gRPC is not a natural choice for public APIs because browsers cannot speak the binary HTTP/2 framing directly.
for real-time bidirectional or server-push scenarios (chat, live dashboards, order tracking). REST's request/response model cannot natively push updates to clients.
when you are integrating with legacy enterprise systems or financial/government services that require its formal contracts, WS-Security, or transactions.

The canonical approach is to model the domain as a set of resources, design their URIs, map CRUD operations to HTTP methods, choose a representation format (almost always JSON), and implement using a framework that provides HTTP routing.
Step 1: Identify resources. Resources are nouns, not verbs. A bookstore API has resources like
Step 2: Design URI hierarchy. Use plural nouns and nesting to represent relationships. A book's reviews live at
. Keep hierarchies shallow — more than two levels of nesting becomes unwieldy.
Step 3: Map HTTP methods. GET reads, POST creates subordinates, PUT replaces, PATCH partially updates, DELETE removes. Never use GET for operations that change state.
Step 4: Choose status codes precisely. Return 201 Created (with a
header) when a resource is created. Return 204 No Content for successful DELETEs. Return 422 Unprocessable Entity for validation failures, not 400.
Step 5: Implement with a framework. The following example uses FastAPI (Python), which auto-generates OpenAPI documentation and enforces Pydantic validation.
Simple example: calling a REST API with Python requests

import requests

BASE_URL = "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com"

# GET — fetch a single resource
response = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/posts/1")
print(response.status_code)  # 200
post = response.json()
print(post["title"])

# POST — create a new resource
new_post = {"title": "REST in Practice", "body": "REST is an architecture...", "userId": 1}
response = requests.post(f"{BASE_URL}/posts", json=new_post)
print(response.status_code)  # 201
print(response.json()["id"])

# PUT — full replacement
replacement = {"id": 1, "title": "Updated Title", "body": "New body", "userId": 1}
response = requests.put(f"{BASE_URL}/posts/1", json=replacement)
print(response.status_code)  # 200

# PATCH — partial update
response = requests.patch(f"{BASE_URL}/posts/1", json={"title": "Patched Title"})
print(response.status_code)  # 200

# DELETE — remove a resource
response = requests.delete(f"{BASE_URL}/posts/1")
print(response.status_code)  # 200 (this mock API returns 200, real should be 204)
Realistic example: building a paginated list endpoint with filtering

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional
import math

app = FastAPI()

# Simulated data store
PRODUCTS = [
    {"id": i, "name": f"Product {i}", "category": "electronics" if i % 2 == 0 else "books",
     "price": round(10.0 + i * 1.5, 2)}
    for i in range(1, 101)
]


class PaginatedResponse(BaseModel):
    items: list[dict]
    total: int
    page: int
    per_page: int
    total_pages: int
    next_url: Optional[str]
    prev_url: Optional[str]


@app.get("/products", response_model=PaginatedResponse)
def list_products(
    page: int = Query(1, ge=1, description="Page number (1-based)"),
    per_page: int = Query(20, ge=1, le=100, description="Items per page"),
    category: Optional[str] = Query(None, description="Filter by category"),
    min_price: Optional[float] = Query(None, ge=0),
    max_price: Optional[float] = Query(None, ge=0),
):
    """
    GET /products?page=2&per_page=10&category=electronics&min_price=20
    Demonstrates pagination, filtering, and self-descriptive HATEOAS links.
    """
    # Apply filters
    filtered = PRODUCTS
    if category:
        filtered = [p for p in filtered if p["category"] == category]
    if min_price is not None:
        filtered = [p for p in filtered if p["price"] >= min_price]
    if max_price is not None:
        filtered = [p for p in filtered if p["price"] <= max_price]

    total = len(filtered)
    total_pages = max(1, math.ceil(total / per_page))

    if page > total_pages and total > 0:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Page not found")

    start = (page - 1) * per_page
    items = filtered[start : start + per_page]

    base = f"/products?per_page={per_page}"
    next_url = f"{base}&page={page + 1}" if page < total_pages else None
    prev_url = f"{base}&page={page - 1}" if page > 1 else None

    return {
        "items": items,
        "total": total,
        "page": page,
        "per_page": per_page,
        "total_pages": total_pages,
        "next_url": next_url,
        "prev_url": prev_url,
    }

Stripe's payment API is widely studied as a production-quality REST design. Every resource — Charge, Customer, PaymentIntent, Refund — has a unique ID and lives under a stable URI. The API version is embedded in the URL path (
) to allow breaking changes without disrupting existing integrations. Idempotency keys solve the double-charge problem: clients pass an
header, and the server guarantees that retrying an identical request returns the same result rather than creating a duplicate charge.
GitHub's REST API demonstrates mature resource modeling at scale. Repositories, issues, pull requests, and commits are all resources with discoverable URIs. Rate limiting uses standard headers (
) so clients can self-throttle. Pagination uses
— an implementation of the HATEOAS constraint.

import requests
import time

GITHUB_TOKEN = "your_personal_access_token"
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
    "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
    "X-GitHub-Api-Version": "2022-11-28",
}

def get_all_repos(org: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Paginate through all repositories in a GitHub org using HATEOAS Link headers.
    Demonstrates real-world REST consumption: pagination, rate-limit handling.
    """
    url = f"https://api.github.com/orgs/{org}/repos?per_page=100"
    repos = []

    while url:
        response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)

        # Respect rate limits
        remaining = int(response.headers.get("X-RateLimit-Remaining", 1))
        if remaining == 0:
            reset_at = int(response.headers.get("X-RateLimit-Reset", time.time()))
            sleep_for = max(0, reset_at - int(time.time())) + 1
            print(f"Rate limited. Sleeping {sleep_for}s...")
            time.sleep(sleep_for)
            continue

        response.raise_for_status()
        repos.extend(response.json())

        # Follow HATEOAS next link
        link_header = response.headers.get("Link", "")
        url = None
        for part in link_header.split(","):
            if 'rel="next"' in part:
                url = part.split(";")[0].strip().strip("<>")
                break

    return repos

# repos = get_all_repos("python")
# print(f"Found {len(repos)} repositories")

# Wrong — verb in URI path
GET /getUser/42
POST /createOrder
DELETE /deleteProduct/7

# Correct — nouns only; HTTP method carries the verb
GET /users/42
POST /orders
DELETE /products/7
Storing state on the server between requests

A common stateful anti-pattern is storing the "current user" or "current cart" in a server-side session keyed by a cookie. When you scale to multiple server instances, sessions stored in memory on one server are not visible to other servers, so requests from the same client land on different servers and fail. The REST fix is to pass all authentication context (a JWT or bearer token) in every request and reconstruct the client's context from that token on each call.

# PUT requires the FULL resource representation.
# Sending a partial object silently erases omitted fields.
requests.put("/users/42", json={"email": "new@example.com"})
# Result: name, phone, preferences all wiped to null/default

# PATCH applies only the fields you send.
requests.patch("/users/42", json={"email": "new@example.com"})
# Result: only email is changed; all other fields unchanged
Use nouns, not verbs, in URI paths.
Resource paths identify things; HTTP methods describe what to do to them.
. Collections and individual items share the base path:
Version your API from day one.
Include the version in the URI (
header. Changing a public API without versioning breaks all existing clients.
Return the created resource in POST responses.
A 201 response should include the newly created resource in the body and a
header pointing to its canonical URI.
Never return an unbounded list. Use
query parameters or cursor-based pagination for large datasets. Include total counts and navigation links.
Use ETag and conditional requests for caching.
(a hash of the response body) and honor
in subsequent GET requests. Clients can skip download if content has not changed, receiving only a 304.
Agree on a single error response format (RFC 9457 Problem Details is the standard) and use it everywhere. Clients should be able to handle all errors with the same code path.
FastAPI, Django REST Framework, and most modern frameworks auto-generate OpenAPI specifications. Keep it accurate and publish it — it is the primary integration contract.

The stateless constraint is the foundation of REST's scalability story. Because no per-client state lives on the server, load balancers can route requests to any available instance without sticky sessions. When traffic grows, more server instances are added and they all handle requests equally, with no synchronization between them.
HTTP caching drastically reduces origin server load for read-heavy APIs. The three primary caching mechanisms are:
— the response can be served from cache for N seconds without revalidation. Suitable for infrequently changing data like product catalogues or country lists.
— the server returns a hash of the response. On subsequent requests, the client sends the hash; if unchanged, the server responds 304 Not Modified with no body, saving bandwidth.
— similar mechanism using a timestamp instead of a hash.

Pagination controls response size. A collection endpoint without pagination may return millions of records if not constrained. Cursor-based pagination (returning an opaque cursor to the next page) is preferable to offset-based for large datasets because it remains stable as data is inserted or deleted and has O(1) query cost using an indexed cursor column rather than O(n) SQL

Connection pooling and HTTP/2 multiplexing reduce the overhead of multiple round trips. With HTTP/2, multiple REST requests can be issued in parallel over a single TCP connection, eliminating the per-request TLS and TCP handshake costs of HTTP/1.1.

With HTTP/2 multiplexing, the DNS and TLS costs are amortized across all requests, and the requests execute in parallel, so the sum reduces to approximately the latency of the slowest single request rather than the sum of all.
— async, auto-generates OpenAPI, uses Pydantic for validation. Best choice for new Python REST services.
— batteries-included, integrates tightly with Django ORM, mature serializer and permission system.
— lightweight, good for small services or when full Django is unnecessary overhead.
— FastAPI and DRF generate OpenAPI specs automatically. Swagger UI renders them as interactive documentation.
— GUI tools for building, testing, and sharing REST API requests. Postman supports environment variables and test scripts.
— command-line HTTP client with a more readable syntax than curl for ad-hoc REST testing.
— for automated testing of FastAPI endpoints without spinning up a real server.

# Testing a FastAPI REST API with pytest + httpx
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from main import app  # import the FastAPI app

client = TestClient(app)

def test_create_book():
    response = client.post("/books", json={
        "title": "Clean Code",
        "author": "Robert C. Martin",
        "isbn": "978-0132350884",
        "year": 2008
    })
    assert response.status_code == 201
    data = response.json()
    assert data["title"] == "Clean Code"
    assert "id" in data

def test_get_book_not_found():
    response = client.get("/books/nonexistent-id")
    assert response.status_code == 404

def test_delete_book():
    # Create then delete
    create_resp = client.post("/books", json={
        "title": "Test Book", "author": "Author", "isbn": "123", "year": 2020
    })
    book_id = create_resp.json()["id"]

    delete_resp = client.delete(f"/books/{book_id}")
    assert delete_resp.status_code == 204

    # Confirm it is gone
    get_resp = client.get(f"/books/{book_id}")
    assert get_resp.status_code == 404

REST APIs are stateless, so credentials must be sent with every request. The standard approach is Bearer token authentication using JSON Web Tokens (JWT). The client obtains a signed token from an auth endpoint, then passes it in the
header on all subsequent requests. The server validates the signature and claims without hitting a session store.
Production REST APIs must limit request rates to prevent abuse and protect downstream resources. The standard response headers for rate limiting are
(calls left in the current window), and
(Unix timestamp when the window resets). When the limit is exceeded, return 429 Too Many Requests with a
header telling the client when to retry.

All inputs arriving over the network must be treated as untrusted. Use a validation library (Pydantic in Python) to enforce type constraints, length limits, and format rules before any data reaches your database or business logic. Parameterize all database queries — never interpolate user input into SQL strings. Validate content types: reject requests that claim to send JSON but pass malformed payloads with a 415 Unsupported Media Type or 400.

REST APIs must be served exclusively over HTTPS in production. TLS encrypts the payload (protecting tokens and personal data from network eavesdroppers) and authenticates the server (preventing man-in-the-middle attacks). Configure HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) so browsers always use HTTPS even if the user enters
. Set appropriate CORS headers to restrict which origins can call the API from the browser.

Think of a REST API as a warehouse with a universal interface. Every item in the warehouse has a unique shelf address (the URI). Any visitor — regardless of language, country, or background — uses the same five actions to interact with the warehouse: look at an item (GET), add a new item (POST), replace an item entirely (PUT), update an item in place (PATCH), or remove an item (DELETE). The warehouse never remembers your previous visits — each time you arrive you must present your membership card (authentication token) and your shopping list (the full request).

The warehouse can store its most popular items near the entrance for fast retrieval (caching). Delivery trucks (intermediary proxies) can shuttle goods between the loading dock and the warehouse floor without either the visitor or the warehouse needing to know how many trucks are involved or what routes they take (layered system). The shelving system (the URI hierarchy) organizes items so that related items live in the same section: all books are in the /books aisle, and the reviews for book 42 are on the /books/42/reviews shelf.

This mental model highlights what REST makes simple (read and write of named resources through a fixed vocabulary) and what it makes harder (querying across many resources at once, streaming, or actions that do not map to CRUD — use GraphQL or gRPC for those).
REST is an architectural style defined by six constraints — client-server, stateless, cacheable, uniform interface, layered system, and code on demand — not a specification or protocol.
Statelessness is the principal scalability mechanism: any server can handle any request because no session context lives on the server between requests.
The uniform interface constraint (standard HTTP methods + URIs + status codes) is why REST APIs are accessible to any HTTP client without client-specific libraries or WSDL generation.

Use HTTP methods and status codes with their specified semantics. GET must be safe and idempotent; PUT replaces fully; PATCH updates partially; DELETE is idempotent; POST is neither safe nor idempotent.
retrieves it. Never put verbs in URI paths.
Cache-Control, ETag, and conditional request headers are REST's performance multipliers — use them on all read endpoints to enable CDN and browser caching.
REST's primary weaknesses are over-fetching, under-fetching, and multiple round trips for related data. GraphQL addresses these at the cost of a more complex query layer.
Secure a REST API with HTTPS, Bearer token authentication (JWT), precise authorization checks per endpoint, input validation, rate limiting, and correct CORS configuration.
